rationale: In the theory setup, the 2011 paper explicitly cites Kivetz et al. (2006) as evidence for the established goal-gradient pattern that motivation rises as consumers get closer to a goal. That prior result is then used to motivate the paper's core gap: progress level alone is not enough because perceived velocity also shapes motivation. Although the citation appears with another reference, it has a clear conceptual framing role in positioning the new contribution.
relation_description: The citing paper treats Kivetz et al. (2006) as a baseline finding on progress-based motivation in goal pursuit, especially in loyalty contexts. It builds from that foundation by proposing and testing how perceived velocity interacts with progress level and can reverse which velocity state is motivational across stages.
